coredata.dc:title: Photothermal effect of carbon dots for boosted photothermal-assisted photocatalytic water/seawater splitting into hydrogen
coredata.dc:creator: Lu, Jialin
Shi, Yuxing
Chen, Zhouze
Sun, Xinhai
Yuan, Hao
Guo, Feng
Shi, Weilong
coredata.dc:description: Herein, carbon dots were loaded on the surface of hollow tubular carbon nitride (CDs/TCN) via a two-step route of hydrothermal and calcination processes for photothermal-assisted photocatalytic water/seawater splitting into hydrogen full spectrum light irradiation. The introduction of CDs promotes the separation and transfer rate of photogenerated carriers, and provides active sites for the hydrogen precipitation reaction. Most importantly, with the assistance of the thermal insulation effect of TCN, the photothermal effect of CDs can greatly increase the surface temperature of CDs/TCN composite system, thus enhancing the photocatalytic H2 production performance. All the synthesized CDs/TCN composites exhibited efficient photocatalytic H2 production rates, the highest of which reached 12.94 mmol h−1 g−1 and 11.92 mmol h−1 g−1 from water and seawater splitting, respectively. This study provides a new idea for enhancing the photothermal-assisted photocatalytic water splitting into hydrogen using CDs with photothermal effect.

head.ce:title: Photothermal effect of carbon dots for boosted photothermal-assisted photocatalytic water/seawater splitting into hydrogen

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, carbon dots were loaded on the surface of hollow tubular carbon nitride (CDs/TCN) via a two-step route of hydrothermal and calcination processes for photothermal-assisted photocatalytic water/seawater splitting into hydrogen full spectrum light irradiation. The introduction of CDs promotes the separation and transfer rate of photogenerated carriers, and provides active sites for the hydrogen precipitation reaction. Most importantly, with the assistance of the thermal insulation effect of TCN, the photothermal effect of CDs can greatly increase the surface temperature of CDs/TCN composite system, thus enhancing the photocatalytic H2 production performance. All the synthesized CDs/TCN composites exhibited efficient photocatalytic H2 production rates, the highest of which reached 12.94 mmol h−1 g−1 and 11.92 mmol h−1 g−1 from water and seawater splitting, respectively. This study provides a new idea for enhancing the photothermal-assisted photocatalytic water splitting into hydrogen using CDs with photothermal effect.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Photocatalytic (PC) water or seawater splitting into H2 is considered an effective method to develop and transform renewable solar energy [1–3]. In the PC system, photocatalysts, contribute a huge role in the highly efficient solar-hydrogen conversion process [4–6]. However, most semiconductor photocatalysts have some disadvantages, such as poor light absorption capacity, slow charge transfer rate, so various methods are needed to modify semiconductors to improve their photocatalytic activity [7–10].

Carbon materials, especially carbon dots (CDs) that bind to semiconductors, are often used to modify semiconductors due to their strong light absorption capacity, up-conversion photoluminescence property, low toxicity, and high stability, making them very widely studied in the field of photocatalysis [11–14]. For instance, Guo et al have used CDs to enhance the photo-absorption capacity of oxygen-deficient ZnO, thus enhancing the photocatalytic degradation activity of tetracycline under visible light irradiation [15]. Due to the rapid photo-induced electron transfer nature, CDs can effectively bind photo-generated charge, catalytically activate PMS, and realize efficient purification of organic pollutants in water [16]. Furthermore, Shi et al., synthesized CDs-CoO composite photocatalyst and showed high photo for photocatalytic stability compared to pure phase CoO due to the existence of metal–oxygen-carbon bonds via the combination of CDs with CoO [17]. In addition to the above features, CDs have excellent photothermal conversion properties and have been extensively studied in the medical field in recent years [18]. For example, Geng et al have used the photothermal effect of CDs in composite with WS2 for the treatment of osteosarcoma and bone-tissue engineering, where both CDs have excellent photothermal conversion properties with WS2 [19]. However, currently, relatively few studies discuss the dominant role of the photothermal effect of CDs in photocatalytic field and research on charge transport at the interfaces of CDs and semiconductor, which helps to understand of the essence of the photocatalytic reaction and improve the overall charge separation and photocatalytic activity.

As a metal-free semiconductor, graphitic carbon nitride (CN) is widely used in photocatalytic research because of its advantages of non-toxic and easy preparation [20–24]. However, the hydrogen production rate of ordinary CN is too low due to the lack of visible light absorption capacity and the low specific surface area [25–29]. Further improvement of semiconductor photocatalysts’ activity by controlling morphology has been shown to be effective [30–32]. Among them, for the modification of the CN, the construction of hollow structure can not only increase the light absorption capacity of the photocatalyst, but also displayed a certain thermal insulation effect [33–35]. In particular, the one-dimensional hollow tubular CN (TCN) has received more attention in recent years [36]. The hollow tubular structure effectively shortens the volume surface transmission distance of the material and significantly improves the transmission rate of photon-generated carrier [37]. In addition, the incident light through this unique hollow tubular structure occurs multiple light scattering/reflection in the inner cavity, greatly enhancing the ability of light capture [38,39]. Meanwhile, a large specific surface area of TCN can provide an abundance of reaction sites to accelerate the forward progress of the photocatalytic reaction [40]. Consequently, CDs and TCN are combined to build an CDs/TCN composite system, which can effectively improve the temperature of the system and realize the photothermal-assisted synergistic photocatalytic effect.

With this mind, herein, CDs were coated on the surface of TCN to form CDs/TCN composites by a facile two-step method of hydrothermal and calcination processes. With the aid of the photothermal effect of CDs, the temperature of CDs/TCN composite system is increased, which accelerates the charge transfer efficiency. In addition, hollow tubular structures of TCN can reduce the heat loss in the composite system and increase the light utilization of light due to the multiple photon reflections. Under the synergistic effect, the CDs/TCN composite photocatalyst exhibited a superior photocatalytic H2 evolution activity from water/seawater splitting under full-spectrum light irradiation. This work presents a simple, feasible, and effective strategy to modify semiconductor for enhancing photocatalytic performance by using the CDs-based photothermal materials.

Carbon dots (CDs) were prepared by an electrochemical route of etching graphite rods [41]. Ultra-pure water was used as the electrolyte, and two graphite rods were used as the cathode and anode respectively. A voltage of 30 V was applied to both electrodes and the electrolyte was stirred. After 10 days of continuous stirring, the solution gradually changed to black. The obtained solution was filtered through a double layered filter paper and centrifuged at high speed (10000 r/min) for 30 min to remove the large particle size of carbon particles. After freeze-drying, CDs powders were redispersed in aqueous solution at a concentration of 2 mg/mL.

Two raw chemicals of urea (CO(NH2)2) (A.R. ≥ 98 %) and melamine (C3N3(NH2)3) (A.R. ≥ 99 %) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. and analytical grade without further purification. 8 g of urea and 5 g of melamine were mixed and added to 70 mL of deionized water. The solution obtained from the mixing was magnetic stirred for 3 h. The obtained solution was poured into the PTFE hydrothermal reactor, heated to 180 ℃ and kept for 20 h. Then, after natural cooling to mutual room temperature, the supernatant was removed and the precipitate was retained. Finally, the resulting precipitate was washed twice with water, once with alcohol and then dried at 60 ℃. After the precursor is completely dried, it is placed in the crucible and calcined at 550 ℃ for 4 h at a heating rate of 5 ℃/min in the air atmosphere. After natural cooling, the TCN powder was obtained with the yield of ∼1 % (about 0.13 g).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of carbon dots modified hollow tubular g-C3N4 (CDs/TCN)
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis procedure of CDs/TCN is similar to that of TCN, except that a certain amount of CDs solution was added before the hydrothermal process. The detailed synthetic process is shown in Fig. 1 . According to the mass ratio of CDs to TCN, the samples are named as CD/TCN-1 %, CD/TCN-2.5 %, CD/TCN-3 %, CD/TCN-3.5 % and CD/TCN-4 %.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of carbon dots modified g-C3N4 (CDs/CN) and g-C3N4 (CN)
ce:section.ce:para: For comparison, CDs/CN was prepared by placing 8 g urea and fixed mass ratio of the CDs (3.5 wt%) to CN in a crucible and calcined at 550 ℃ for 4 h at 5℃/min in an air atmosphere. The pure CN was obtained in the same way without adding CDs solution.

The specific experimental procedures of characterizations, photoelectrochemical properties measurements, photocatalytic H2 evolution tests and photothermal performance measurements were listed in the Supporting Information.

A schematic representation of the synthesis of the CDs/TCN was exhibited in Fig. 1. First, the CDs synthesized through the electrochemical etching of graphite rods were mixed with urea/melamine in aqueous solution. During mixing, melamine can produce cyanic acid by hydrolysis, which in turn can form a tubular precursor with urea in the hydrothermal process, while the CDs can be adsorbed to the surface of tubular precursors [42–44]. Subsequently, in the process of calcination, large amounts of gases, such as ammonia, carbon dioxide, etc, produced at high temperature transform the solid tubular structure into a hollow tubular structure, and strong interfacial interactions make CDs loaded on the surface of the TCN to form the CDs/TCN composite [43].

The morphology and elemental composition of as-prepared photocatalysts were investigated by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM). Fig. S1a exhibits TCN possesses the hollow tubular structure with smooth outer wall, and its inner diameter is about 2 μm and length size is approx. 8 μm. After coating CDs on the surface of TCN to form CDs/TCN composites, the morphology of them remains hollow tubular structure (Fig. 2 a and Fig. S1b-e), which is identical with pure TCN, indicating CDs have no effect on the morphology changes of TCN. From TEM image in Fig. 2b, the tubular wall thickness of CDs/TCN can be measured at about 50 nm based on the edge of deep color. In addition, the contrast of the light and dark regions also further determines the hollow structure, which is conducive to the uniform distribution of CDs in the inner and outer walls of the tube-like TCN. Furthermore, this hollow structure not only increases the specific surface area and light absorption rate of photocatalyst but also has a certain thermal insulation effect, which can effectively promote the photothermal synergistic catalytic performance [45,46]. Further observations revealed that various pores with uneven size of about 60 nm can be found on the surface of CDs/TCN in irregular distribution, which is favorable for the photo-induced charge transfer [47]. To determine the successful presence of CDs, high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) was analyzed and displayed in Fig. 2c. It can be seen that CDs with the size of 2–5 nm are evenly distributed inside the inner and outer channels of TCN wall, and in the HRTEM image of Fig. 2d, lattice fringes of CDs can be measured to be 0.21, which is corresponding to the (100) crystallographic plane of carbon, demonstrating that CDs were successfully introduced in the CDs/TCN composite [42,48]. More importantly, the CDs with high crystallinity and small size can be considered as the active sites and light-induced heat source for photo-thermal catalytic hydrogen evolution and accelerate the diffusion of electrons [30]. The composition of CDs/TCN was analyzed by elemental mapping and energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) spectroscopy (Fig. 2e, Fig. S2 and Table S1) and revealed that the CDs/TCN composite was composed of three elements: C, N and O.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of the TCN and CDs/TCN with different mass ratios are given in Fig. 3 a1. For the TCN sample, two obvious characteristic diffraction peaks are located at 13.2° and 27.5°, which are corresponded to the plane sequence of tris-triazine units of the (100) plane structure and periodic accumulation along the c-axis of the (002) plane in the network of g-C3N4, respectively [49–51]. In addition, compared with the pure TCN, the XRD patterns of CDs/TCN composites are strikingly similar, which indicated that the original crystal structure of g-C3N4 was preserved after the introduction of CDs. Nevertheless, the peak (100) intensity of CDs/TCN composites becomes more and more weaker with increasing number of CDs in comparison of pristine TCN, which is due to the porous structures of TCN, leading to the periodic disruption of the in-plane structure [42]. In addition, as can be seen in Fig. 3a2, the (002) diffraction peaks of CDs/TCN composites are shifted slightly with increasing CDs, because the CDs adjust the distance of the layer of g-C3N4, reflecting a strong interfacial interaction between the CDs and the TCN [36]. The functional groups of the as-synthesized samples were further determined by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR). Fig. 3b1 shows that the FT-IR spectra of CDs/TCN composites, which agrees well with the typical vibrational modes of TCN, indicating the addition of CDs has not been changed the molecular structure of TCN. The strong vibrational bands in the range of 1200–1700 cm−1 correspond to unique stretching modes of the CN heterocycle ring, and the broad band at 3000–3500 cm−1 can be associated with amino residues, NH components and hydroxyl groups adsorbed on the surface of TCN [52,53]. The fluctuating signal at 805 cm−1 is thought to be caused by the bending vibration peak of tri-s-triazine ring in the network of g-C3N4 [54,55]. From Fig. 3b2, the signal intensity gradually decreases with increasing CDs amounts in CDs/TCN composites and shifts evidently to a lower wave number region in contrast of pristine TCN, indicating the introduction of CDs produces a strong interaction of the heterogeneous interface between CDs and TCN.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) survey spectra in Fig. 4 a reveal that the three main elements contained in TCN and CDs/TCN-3.5 % are composed of C, N and O. The increase in the surface atomic ratio of C and N elements in CDs/TCN-3.5 % relative to TCN is due to the different ratio of CDs contained in CDs/TCN-3.5 % composite (Table S2), which is consistent with the EDX results (Table S1). In order to better analyze the composition of the photocatalyst, the narrow-scan XPS spectra of three elements were investigated. In Fig. 4b, three major peaks at 284.8, 285.9 and 288.2 eV were detected in the C 1s spectrum for TCN, representing CC, CNH and C-NC, respectively [56–58]. Compared with pure TCN, CDs/TCN-3.5 % exhibited one new peak of CO bond at 288.9 eV due to the abundant hydroxyl groups of CDs [54]. The high-resolution N 1s spectrum of TCN in Fig. 4c can be divided into four peaks at 398.8, 399.6, 400.8 and 404.3 eV, which are attributed to the C-NC, N-(C)3 group, NH bond and π-excited state, respectively [36,59,60]. Combining Fig. 4b and c, it is clearly seen that the positions of C 1s and N 1s peaks over CDs/TCN have been changed significantly in comparison of TCN, indicating the introduction of CDs can change the electron density on the TCN surface, thus forming chemical bonds between two materials [61]. As exhibited in Fig. 4d, two peaks in O 1s spectrum were detected in CDs/TCN with containing 531.8 eV for CO and 533.4 eV for COH from CDs, respectively [62]. As for the OH signals detected in TCN (532.2 and 533.5 eV) are thought to be caused by adsorbed molecules of oxygen-containing elements such as oxygen and water [63].

To investigate the effect of CDs on the optical properties of the TCN, the UV–vis diffuse spectra were employed. As exhibited in Fig. 5 a, the pure TCN showed poor absorption capacity in the visible light region with an absorption edge of about 463 nm. After the introduction of CDs, the absorption edge has a significant redshift, increasing from 463 to 489 nm, indicating that the strong photo-response range of CDs promotes the light absorption capacity of the composite in the visible region [64]. In Fig. S3, the color of the sample was gradually deepened as the amount of CDs increases, which again proves that CDs indeed enhances the light absorption of TCN, which is consistent with test result in the Fig. 5a. Subsequently, the band gaps of the samples were obtained using the Kubelk-Munk method. In Fig. 5b, the band gap of the pristine TCN is about 2.72 eV, which limits the light absorption capacity of the TCN in the visible region. As the number of CDs introduced increased in the composites, the band gap width of the as-prepared CDs/TCN samples decreased from 2.72 to 2.52 eV, caused by the heterostructure between the TCN and CDs [42]. To further study the band structure of TCN and CDs/TCN, the Mott-Schottky (M−S) plots at three frequencies of 800, 1000 and 1200 Hz were measured. In Fig. 5a and Fig. S4, the slopes of the M−S plots for all samples are positive, which is characteristic of the n-type semiconductor. The flat band potential of the CDs/TCN-3.5 % shifts in a more negative direction compared to the TCN, suggesting that the CDs can regulate the position of the conduction band (CB), thus enhancing the thermodynamic driving force of the reaction for photocatalytic water splitting into hydrogen. The CB positions of TCN and CDs/TCN-3.5 % are observed at −0.74 and −0.77 V, respectively. Based on the measured CB position and band gap, the valance band (VB) positions can be calculated, and the band structure maps of TCN and CDs/TCN-3.5 % composite can be drawn in Fig. 5d, and both TCN and CDs/TCN-3.5 % meet the reaction conditions for the photocatalytic hydrogen evolution reaction.

To evaluate its photocatalytic activity, the hydrogen evolution curves of TCN and CDs/TCN composites were investigated under 300 W Xenon lamp irradiation conditions at room temperature. As exhibited in Fig. 6 a, pure TCN possesses the photocatalytic activity and as the irradiation light time increases, the hydrogen production gradually increases. Excitingly, all the samples with loaded with CDs displayed the higher photocatalytic H2 evolution than the pristine TCN, and the CDs/TCN-3.5 % showed the highest hydrogen production performance, indicating that CDs can effectively improve the photocatalytic activity of TCN because CDs increases the active site on the surface of TCN and promotes carrier separation and transfer. The rate of hydrogen production for samples in photocatalytic water splitting reaction is given in Fig. 6b, with the highest one being CDs/TCN-3.5 %, reaching the 12.94 mmol g−1 h−1, approximately 1.53 times that of TCN, which is superior compared with other carbon nitride-based photocatalysts (Table S3). As we known, the seawater resources are richer for the earth than for freshwater resources, thus photocatalytic seawater splitting is more practical value. To explore the effect of seawater on the CDs/TCN-3.5 % photocatalytic activity, the CDs/TCN-3.5 % photocatalytic hydrogen production in the simulated seawater was measured by gas chromatograph. In Fig. 6c and d, the CDs/TCN-3.5 % hydrogen production capacity in seawater remained stable, at about 11.92 mmol g−1 h−1. The results show that loading a suitable mass of CDs on the surface of TCN can effectively improve its photocatalytic activity, while maintaining a high photocatalytic hydrogen production rate in seawater, which is important in practical applications.

To investigate the photothermal effect of CDs on the photocatalytic hydrogenation process, the photocatalytic hydrogen activities of TCN and CDs/TCN-3.5 % were tested and contrasted at different temperatures within 2 h irradiation. As can be seen from Fig. 7 a and b, the as-prepared TCN and CDs/TCN-3.5 % exhibit different photocatalytic activities under different temperature conditions, and it decreases with the reducing temperature, indicating temperature parameters play a certain role in this system. Thus, the temperature changes of the powder of the as-synthesized samples in water under light irradiation were firstly explored and recorded using an infrared thermal imaging camera. Temperature changes of the CDs/TCN-3.5 % and TCN within 8 min under a 300 W Xenon lamp illumination are shown in Fig. S5. The temperature of CDs/TCN-3.5 % powder rapidly rose from 30.2 to 103 ℃ and remained stable, while TCN rose from 32.0 to only 89.4 ℃. Obviously, it is obtained that the temperature difference of the CDs/TCN-3.5 % composite material is significantly higher than that in the pristine TCN under the same light conditions, which proves that the CDs has a strong photothermal effect that could effectively increase the temperature of the photocatalytic reaction system of TCN. In addition, considering that the photocatalytic reaction was performed in an aqueous solution system, the liquid temperature changes during the photocatalytic water splitting reaction were also recorded using an infrared thermal imaging camera. As shown in Fig. 7c, the liquid temperature in TCN photocatalytic water splitting system was increased from 8.6 to 36.7 ℃ after 2 h irradiation, while for the CDs/TCN-3.5 %, the final temperature is up to 40.5 ℃ under same conditions, indicating the addition of CDs can induce photothermal effects, resulting in an increase in local surface temperature (Fig. S6). To demonstrate that the photothermal effect of CDs in seawater can still promote the photocatalytic activity, the above experimental procedures were repeated in simulated seawater. As presented in Fig. 7d and e, the activity of the photocatalysts in seawater displayed similar trends to that in water. Analogously, the liquid temperature of TCN and CDs/TCN-3.5 % in seawater rose to 36.6 ℃ and 39.0 ℃ after 2 h photocatalytic reaction, respectively (Fig. S7 and Fig. 7f), which is similar to the results tested in water. Thus, the photothermal effect of CDs can effectively promote the photocatalytic water/seawater splitting into hydrogen in CDs/TCN composite system.

For photothermal-assisted photocatalysis, it is necessary to reduce the thermal loss in the system. Therefore, the thermal insulation properties of the prepared CDs/TCN-3.5 % should be studied. The experimental operation was to heat CDs/TCN-3.5 % sample by a 300 W Xenon lamp illumination for 8 min, then naturally cool naturally for 8 min, and record the photocatalyst surface temperature at 1 every min interval. As exhibited in Fig. 8 a, after turning off the light source for 8 min, the temperature of CDs/TCN-3.5 % decreased to 31.4 ℃ and tend to be stable, which is about 5.4℃ higher than room temperature, indicating that CDs/TCN possesses excellent thermal insulation properties. In Fig. 8b, the temperature change curve of CDs/TCN-3.5 % is presented, and calculated its average cooling rate is about 1.47 ℃/min, and the slow cooling rate further reflects the excellent insulation properties of CDs/TCN-3.5 %. Theoretically, for hollow tubular structures, the tube wall can act as thermal resistance to reduce heat transfer between inside and outside the tube. Thus, it is assumed that its excellent thermal insulation performance is due to its hollow structure. To confirm the hypothesis, the above experimental procedure was repeated for CDs/CN-3.5 % with coating the same CDs contents on the ordinary morphology bulk CN and used to contrast with CDs/TCN-3.5 %. As shown in Fig. 8a and b, after 8 min of natural cooling, the final temperature of CDs/CN-3.5 % was 28.4℃, significantly lower than CDs/TCN-3.5 %, indicating that the heat insulation property of CDs/CN-3.5 % are weaker than its for CDs/TCN-3.5 %, and the CDs/CN-3.5 % exhibited a faster cooling rate (1.59 ℃/min), which further demonstrated the better thermal insulation effect of the hollow tubular structures. Compared to CDs/CN-3.5 %, it can be observed a faster heating rate and a higher final temperature for CDs/TCN-3.5 % under light conditions since the hollow tubular structure reduces the heat loss of the photocatalyst. For the sake of contrast, the insulation performance of pure TCN was investigated in Fig. S8, which can also show the agreement with this role of hollow tubular structure. In addition, the light can be refracted and reflected multiple times in the hollow tube structure, thus improving the photon utilization of the CDs inside the TCN, which is also another reason for the high photothermal conversion efficiency of the CDs/TCN-3.5 % [65]. Consequently, in the CDs/TCN photocatalytic water/seawater splitting system, the hollow tubular structures can effectively promote the photocatalytic activity, through cooperate with the photothermal effects of CDs by reducing heat loss and promoting light absorption capacity.

As displayed in Fig. 9 a, the apparent quantum efficiency (AQE) of as-prepared CDs/TCN-3.5 % in water and seawater was 9.8 % and 7.8 % under 420 nm light, respectively. To explore the stability of the CDs/TCN-3.5 %, four-cycle hydrogen production experiments were performed, and the experimental results are shown in Fig. 9b. The experimental results show that the photothermal-assisted photocatalytic activity of CDs/TCN-3.5 % in water and seawater is almost unchanged after four rounds of photocatalytic hydrogen production. In Fig. 9c and d, the XRD patterns of the post-reactive CDs/TCN-3.5 % remained largely unchanged and its hollow tubular structure was not disrupted, which again illustrates the high stability of the CDs/TCN composite. The high AQE and stability further illustrate the practical significance of this study and the great potential application prospect of the photothermal effect of CDs in the field of photocatalysis.

As exhibited in Fig. 10 a, the N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms of all the samples showed the typical type IV with H3 hysteresis loop, indicating that the existence of pores in samples, which are consistent with the result of TEM image (Fig. 2b). Due to the porous hollow tubular structure, the resulted large specific surface area in both TCN and CDs/TCN-3.5 % samples are higher than that of CN (32 m2 g−1). In addition, the introduction of CDs in CDs/TCN composite can further increase the specific surface area of the TCN, which can provide more active sites for reaction, facilitating the photocatalytic activity. Although the specific surface area of CDs/TCN is larger compared to TCN, this is not the whole reason for its higher photocatalytic activity. Therefore, the effect of photothermal effect of CDs on photon-generated separation and transfer carriers of TCN was explored. As shown in Fig. 10b, compared to the pure TCN, the decreased PL signal of CDs/TCN-3.5 % RT reflect that the loading of CDs is beneficial for the separation of photoexcited charge carriers. And an even bigger drop in PL signal of CDs/TCN-3.5 % 50℃ can be observed compared to the CDs/TCN-3.5 % RT, indicating the increasing of temperature can further enhance the charge separation efficiency. Subsequently, the effect of temperature on photon-generated carrier transfer was further investigated by photocurrent response curves and electrochemical impedance (EIS) spectra at different temperatures. In Fig. 10c, compared with pure TCN, CDs/TCN-3.5 %-RT, CDs/TCN-3.5 %-50℃ showed better photocurrent response intensity with increasing ambient temperature, further indicating that the higher temperature can further facilitate the transfer of electrons, thus boosting the photocatalytic hydrogen production activity. For the EIS spectra in Fig. 10d, the corresponding equivalent circuit is simulated in the inset of Fig. 10d, where RS and Rct are electrolyte solution resistance and interfacial charge transfer resistance/electrolyte, respectively. In addition, the smallest EIS arc radius of CDs/TCN-3.5 %-50℃ reflect that the introduction of CDs and higher temperature can reduce electrical resistance, which can effectively inhibit the recombination of photo-induced electron-hole pairs.

To further demonstrate the photothermal effect of as-prepared CDs/TCN composite, the photothermal conversion properties of pure water were tested for contrast. As shown in Fig. S9, without the absence of a photocatalyst, the temperature of the liquid in the reactor reached only 28.0 ℃, demonstrating the strong photothermal conversion performance of as-prepared CDs/TCN composite, which further side reflects the photothermal effect of CDs. Based on the above analysis, Fig. 11 demonstrates a possible mechanism for photothermal-assisted photocatalytic hydrogen production using CDs/TCN photocatalyst. During the sunlight, the TCN can be excited to produce photo-induced electrons and holes, and located at the CB and VB, respectively. Supported by the photothermal effect of the CDs, the temperature of the CDs/TCN composite system can be effectively raised, which can facilitate electron separation and transfer. Besides, the thermal insulation effect of the hollow tubular structure of the TCN can effectively reduce the heat loss. Synchronously, the electrons at the CB of the TCN are rapidly transferred to the CDs due to the photo-induced electron transfer properties, and are captured by the H+ in the water to generate H2 from water/seawater splitting. The holes at the VB of the TCN are oxidized by TEOA in solution, thereby further inhibiting the charges’ recombination.

In summary, the CDs/TCN composites with coating CDs on the tubular TCN were synthesized by a simple two-step method of hydrothermal and calcination for achieving high photothermal-assisted photocatalytic hydrogen production activity from water/simulated seawater splitting during the full spectrum light irradiation. A series of characterization results confirmed that the photothermal effect of CDs accelerates the separation and transfer of photo-generated carriers of TCN, while the hollow tubular structures of TCN can reduce the heat loss in the composite system and increase the light utilization of light due to the multiple photon reflections, which contributes to the increasing temperature of the surface temperature of the photocatalyst, thus enhancing the photocatalytic H2 production activity. This study proposes a new scheme to use the CDs with photothermal effect to promote photothermal-assisted photocatalytic hydrogen production of semiconductor.
